title: Egyptian flatbread (Baladi Bread)

overview.description: A rustic Egyptian flatbread with a slightly chewy crumb and a crisp, flour-dusted exterior, traditionally used for scooping up ful medames and other dishes.

ingredients: Extra wholemeal flour or bran, for shaping
method: Place the flours, salt and yeast in a large bowl.
Add the warm water and olive oil if using.
Mix until a rough dough forms.
Knead for 8–10 minutes until smooth and elastic.
Cover the dough and leave to rise for approximately 1 hour, or until doubled in size.
Divide the dough into 6 equal pieces.
Shape each piece into a ball.
Generously dust the work surface with wholemeal flour or bran.
Roll or press each dough ball into a round approximately 15–18cm in diameter.
The breads should be relatively thin but not paper-thin.
Place the breads on baking parchment or a heavily floured tray.
Cover and leave to rest for 20–30 minutes.
Preheat the oven to its highest setting, ideally with a baking stone or heavy baking tray inside.
Place the breads onto the hot surface.
Bake until puffed and lightly browned, approximately 5–8 minutes.
Remove from the oven and cover with a clean tea towel while cooling slightly.

learning.did-you-know: Baladi bread is one of Egypt's most important everyday breads. The word 'baladi' can mean something like 'of the country' or 'local', and the bread is traditionally made with a high proportion of whole wheat flour.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How yeast dough rises and how a very hot oven can cause a flatbread to puff up and form a pocket.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A useful flatbread for scooping up ful medames and other Middle Eastern and North African dishes, while adding an important Egyptian bread to the collection.
chapman-tips: A very hot baking surface helps the bread puff.
Don't roll the breads too thin.
Keep the dough covered while working so it does not dry out.
Wholemeal flour or bran on the outside gives the bread its characteristic rustic finish.
The bread is best served warm.
If the breads do not puff completely, they are still perfectly good for dipping and scooping.
A bread stone or heavy preheated tray helps considerably.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well. Allow the breads to cool completely, stack them with baking parchment between them if desired, and freeze in a sealed bag.
notes.leftovers: Reheat directly from frozen in a toaster, dry frying pan or hot oven.
